  "response": "{
  "judgement": "TP",
  "analysis": "The report is a true positive. The source and sink are correctly identified: `XmlTreeBuilderTest.testDetectCharsetEncodingDeclaration()` opens a `FileInputStream` and does not close it itself. Although `Jsoup.parse(inStream, null, ..., Parser.xmlParser())` usually closes the stream on the normal path, it does not guarantee closure on all exit paths. There is a concrete reachable exceptional path in the callee: `Jsoup.parse` delegates to `DataUtil.load`, which calls `parseInputStream(...)`. In `DataUtil.parseInputStream(...)`, the stream is only closed in the `finally` block if `charsetDoc != null`; however, `charsetDoc` is assigned only after `detectCharset(...)` returns successfully. If `detectCharset(...)` throws before returning, the caller's `FileInputStream` is left unclosed. This is not a shallow or purely theoretical path: `detectCharset(...)` performs actual reads/parsing and can throw `IOException`/`UncheckedIOException`, while it temporarily disables close propagation via `input.allowClose(false)`. Therefore the resource is not always released, which matches CWE-772. Real-world security impact is low because this is test code under `src/test/java` and not a production attack surface, but the leak condition itself is real.",
  "FP_Reason": [],
  "evidence": "Reported code at `src/test/java/org/jsoup/parser/XmlTreeBuilderTest.java:117-118` opens the resource and never closes it locally: `InputStream inStream = new FileInputStream(xmlFile);` then `Document doc = Jsoup.parse(inStream, null, \"http://example.com/\", Parser.xmlParser());`. The call path is `Jsoup.parse(InputStream,...,Parser)` -> `DataUtil.load(in, charsetName, baseUri, parser)` (`src/main/java/org/jsoup/Jsoup.java:283-284`) -> `DataUtil.parseInputStream(...)` (`src/main/java/org/jsoup/helper/DataUtil.java:223-234`). In `DataUtil.parseInputStream(...)`: `CharsetDoc charsetDoc = null; try { charsetDoc = detectCharset(input, charsetName, baseUri, parser); doc = parseInputStream(charsetDoc, baseUri, parser); } finally { if (charsetDoc != null) charsetDoc.input.close(); }`. If `detectCharset(...)` throws before returning, `charsetDoc` stays null and no close happens. Inside `detectCharset(...)` (`DataUtil.java:248-260`), jsoup does `input.allowClose(false); try (Reader reader = new SimpleStreamReader(input, UTF_8)) { doc = parser.parseInput(reader, baseUri); input.reset(); ... } catch (UncheckedIOException e) { throw e.getCause(); } finally { input.allowClose(true); }`. `SimpleStreamReader.close()` calls `in.close()` (`src/main/java/org/jsoup/internal/SimpleStreamReader.java:84-88`), but `ControllableInputStream.close()` only closes when `allowClose` is true (`src/main/java/org/jsoup/internal/ControllableInputStream.java:185-186`). So an exception during charset detection can exit without closing the underlying `FileInputStream`.",

The sink notion is also superficially valid for CWE-772: if the method exits without closing that reader chain, a resource leak could occur. However, on the concrete execution path of this test, the opened reader is passed into `StreamParser.parse(reader, ...)`, and jsoup closes it automatically when parsing finishes. `StreamParser.close()` calls `TreeBuilder.completeParse()`, which calls `CharacterReader.close()`, which closes the original `Reader`; because that original `Reader` is the `BufferedReader` wrapping the `InputStreamReader`, the underlying `InputStreamReader` is closed as well. The test itself asserts that closure happened. So the main reported path is not an actual unclosed-resource bug. A leak is only theoretically possible if an I/O exception aborts parsing before EOF, but this code is a JUnit test under `src/test/java`, reading a fixed local test resource, not shipped library code and not attacker-reachable in real deployments. Therefore this should not be treated as a real-world CWE-772 security vulnerability for jsoup.",

Close path: `StreamParser.java:165-166` -> `treeBuilder.completeParse();`; `TreeBuilder.java:65-69` -> `reader.close(); reader = null;`; `CharacterReader.java:63-69` -> closes its stored `Reader`. Since `TreeBuilder.initialiseParse()` stores the supplied `BufferedReader` in `new CharacterReader(input)` (`TreeBuilder.java:43-53`), closing that `CharacterReader` closes the same `BufferedReader`, which in turn closes the wrapped `InputStreamReader`. The test explicitly checks this at `StreamParserTest.java:329-332`: `assertTrue(isClosed(streamer));` and `assertThrows(IOException.class, reader::ready);`. The file being read comes from test resources via `ParseTest.getFile(\"/htmltests/large.html\")` (`src/test/java/org/jsoup/integration/ParseTest.java:129-132`), confirming this is test-only code over a fixed local file.",

  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The reported resource is `SimpleBufferedInput input = new SimpleBufferedInput(Files.newInputStream(path));` in `src/test/java/org/jsoup/internal/ReaderTest.java:48`. Superficially, `getReader()` returns a `SimpleStreamReader` without an explicit `close()` in that helper, so a local pattern matcher can flag it. However, along the concrete runtime path used by the test, the underlying file stream is consumed to EOF, and `SimpleBufferedInput` is designed to close its wrapped stream automatically at EOF via `closeIfDone(read)`. In the first path, `getString(streamReader)` loops until `streamReader.read(...) == -1`, which causes `SimpleStreamReader.bufferUp()` to call into `ControllableInputStream.read()`, then `SimpleBufferedInput.read()/fill()`, and when EOF is reached `SimpleBufferedInput.closeIfDone(-1)` executes `super.close()`. In the second path, `CharacterReader.consumeTo('X')` falls through to `consumeToEnd()`, which repeatedly buffers until EOF, again driving the same closure path. So the specific source/sink pairing does not produce a real unreleased OS/file descriptor on the tested path. This is also test code, not a production request-handling path, which further reduces any real-world security impact. Therefore this is not a practical CWE-772 issue.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "D1"
  ],
  "evidence": "Relevant code in `ReaderTest.getReader()`:\n`SimpleBufferedInput input = new SimpleBufferedInput(Files.newInputStream(path));`\n`ControllableInputStream stream = ControllableInputStream.wrap(input, 0);`\n`return new SimpleStreamReader(stream, StandardCharsets.UTF_8);`\n\nRelevant close logic in `src/main/java/org/jsoup/internal/SimpleBufferedInput.java`:\n`private void closeIfDone(int read) throws IOException { if (read == -1) { inReadFully = true; super.close(); } }`\nThis method is called from both `read(byte[],...)` and `fill()` after underlying reads.\n\nFirst test path in `ReaderTest`:\n`while ((read = streamReader.read(cbuffer)) != -1) { ... }`\nThis explicitly reads until EOF, which triggers `closeIfDone(-1)` and closes the wrapped `Files.newInputStream(path)`.\n\nSecond test path in `ReaderTest`:\n`CharacterReader cr = new CharacterReader(reader2);`\n`String fullRead = cr.consumeTo('X'); // does not exist in input`\nSince `X` does not exist, `consumeTo(char)` calls `consumeToEnd()`, which keeps reading until EOF. That again reaches `SimpleBufferedInput.closeIfDone(-1)`.\n\nAdditional wrapper close behavior also exists in `SimpleStreamReader.close()`:\n`in.close();`\nand `CharacterReader.close()` calls `reader.close()`, but even without explicit test-side closes, the actual EOF path already closes the underlying file stream.",
